sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gastrointestinal bleeding may arise from the upper or lower GI tract. It can range from self-limited bleeding to life-threatening haemorrhage with shock, aspiration risk, and need for urgent endoscopic or surgical management.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Abdominal pain
- Dizziness or syncope
- Dyspnoea
- Hypotension, pallor, tachycardia, or shock
- Haematemesis or coffee-ground vomit
- Melena
- Haematochezia or maroon stool

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider liver disease, alcohol use, NSAID or anticoagulant exposure, ulcer history, prior variceal bleeding, and recent GI procedures
- All suspected upper GI bleeds require hospital assessment even if bleeding is not active at the time of review
- Some smaller hospitals may not be definitive receiving centres for significant UGIB

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Monitor vital signs frequently
- Have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor
- Position for airway protection and comfort; in active haematemesis or reduced conscious state, lateral positioning may be safer than upright positioning
- Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Gain large bore IV access
- Consider fluids if necessary, titrated to perfusion rather than aggressive normalisation of blood pressure
- Consider antiemetic
- Consider analgesia
- Consider antibiotics only in consultation with on-call doctor, particularly if variceal bleeding is suspected
- Consider PPI administration in consultation with on-call doctor
- Apply ECG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- All patients with suspected UGIB should remain nil by mouth
- Positioning should prioritise airway protection, especially in ongoing haematemesis
- Acute variceal bleeding is associated with higher infection risk, so antibiotic use may be appropriate after senior consultation
- TXA is not routine standard therapy in GI bleeding
